Marriage should be a place of rest, as much as anything on earth can be so, because it is meant to fix affections and form a connection for life. Therefore, it should be entered into with great seriousness, with earnest prayers for direction, for God’s blessing, and with regard to his commands. Parents should carefully advise their children in this important matter, so that things may go well for them as to their souls. It is best for us what is best for our souls. The course Naomi advised seems strange to us; but it was according to the laws and customs of Israel. If the proposed plan had appeared evil, Naomi would not have advised it. Law and custom gave Ruth, who was now converted to the true religion, a legal claim upon Boaz. It was customary for widows to assert this claim. But this is not recorded for imitation in other times, and should not be judged by modern rules. And if there had been any evil in it, Ruth was a woman of too much virtue and too much sense to have listened to it.

What would be improper in one age or nation is not always so in another age or another nation. Being a judge of Israel, Boaz would tell Ruth what she should do; also whether he had the right of redemption, and what methods must be taken, and what rites used, in order to accomplishing her marriage with him or another person. The conduct of Boaz calls for the highest praise. He attempted not to take advantage of Ruth; he did not disdain her as a poor, destitute stranger, nor suspect her of any ill intentions. He spoke honorably of her as a virtuous woman, made her a promise, and as soon as the morning arrived, sent her away with a present to her mother-in-law. Boaz made his promise conditional, for there was a kinsman nearer than he, to whom the right of redemption belonged.

Ruth had done all that was fitting for her to do; she must patiently wait the outcome. Boaz, having taken on this matter, would be sure to manage it well. True believers have even more reason to cast their cares on God, because he has promised to care for them. Our strength is in stillness. This story may encourage us to lay ourselves by faith at the feet of Christ: He is our near Kinsman; having taken our nature upon him. He has the right to redeem. Let us seek to receive from him his directions: Lord, what will you have me to do? He will never blame us for doing this unseasonably. And let us earnestly desire and seek the same rest for our children and friends, that it may be well with them also.
